title: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
author: Nathan Weisser
orientation: Nathan Weisser argues from Scripture (Exodus 18:21, Proverbs 28:17, Psalm 94:20, 2 Corinthians 6:14, Ephesians 5:11) that voting for any pro-abortion candidate, including Trump and Vance, is a sin no matter the context, and answers common objections rooted in pragmatism, Ordo Amoris, and the Dobbs decision.
audiences: Reformed Christian voter weighing 'lesser of two evils' arguments
Republican-leaning pro-life Christian who voted for Trump/Vance
Pastor or church elder counseling congregants on voting
Skeptic who sees voting as a private matter of conscience

questions.question: How does Weisser use the story of Uzzah to frame the question of voting for a pro-abortion candidate?
questions.audience: Reformed Christian voter weighing 'lesser of two evils' arguments
questions.answer: He casts Uzzah as a man caught between two evils—let the Ark fall or touch it—who chose what looked like the lesser evil and was struck dead. The lesson he draws is that no contextual reasoning makes an evil permissible, and 'choosing neither' is always the correct option, which he then applies to voting for a pro-abortion candidate.
questions.quote: The lesson? No amount of context can make an evil permissible. No amount of “good that may come” can allow you to do evil. There is no such thing as the lesser of two evils. “Choosing neither” is always the correct option.

questions.question: What does Weisser mean by labeling Trump, Vance, and similar Republicans 'pro-abortion'?
questions.audience: Republican-leaning pro-life Christian who voted for Trump/Vance
questions.answer: He argues Republicans who once called Democrats pro-abortion for the 'safe, legal, and rare' position now refuse to apply the same label to Republicans holding the same position. For consistency, any candidate who is not planning to curtail abortion in any way is pro-abortion.
questions.quote: When Democrats were pro “safe, legal, and rare” abortion, we all agreed with calling them pro-abortion. Yet, now that prominent Republican candidates are pro “safe, legal, and rare” abortion, we pretend they aren’t. “They aren’t *for* abortion, they just don’t want to do anything about it right now”, folks say. Let’s be consistent here, folks. They’re pro-abortion.

questions.question: How does Exodus 18:21 disqualify pro-abortion candidates in Weisser's argument?
questions.audience: Pastor or church elder counseling congregants on voting
questions.answer: Exodus 18:21 sets four qualifications for civil officers—able, God-fearing, trustworthy, and hating bribes. Weisser claims any pro-abortion candidate fails at least two of these regardless of party affiliation, and that the standard binds democratic voters as the distributed equivalent of Moses' appointing role.
questions.quote: They are to be men who are 1) Able, 2) God-fearing, 3) Trustworthy, and 4) Hate bribes. ***Any* pro-abortion candidate fails at least two of these guidelines,** no matter what letter they have next to their name.

questions.question: Why does Weisser reject the argument that Exodus 18:21 only applies to primary elections, not generals?
questions.audience: Reformed Christian voter weighing 'lesser of two evils' arguments
questions.answer: He uses an analogy: if a church membership were forced to choose between two disqualified elders, the right answer is 'neither'—not picking one. The same logic, he says, applies to a binary general election where both candidates fall short of God's standards.
questions.quote: If that is true, does that not then bind a church membership, if they are given a choice between two elders—both who are disqualified—from exercising the same flawed mindset? No, of course “neither” is always an option.

questions.question: How does Proverbs 28:17 apply to voting for a candidate who refuses to prosecute abortion as murder?
questions.audience: Pastor or church elder counseling congregants on voting
questions.answer: Weisser reads Proverbs 28:17—'no one is to support him'—as a direct prohibition against helping those bloodguilty for shed innocent blood. Casting a vote, he argues, is precisely the act of supporting, helping, and promoting such a man to office.
questions.quote: Yet, isn’t that exactly what a vote is? It’s a political device to *help* him, *support* him, or *promote* him to an office. It’s clear to me that Proverbs has rulers in mind when it speaks of this man, laden with bloodguilt.

questions.question: What is the etymological case Weisser makes for treating a vote as an alliance?
questions.audience: Skeptic who sees voting as a private matter of conscience
questions.answer: He points to the Latin root 'vovere,' meaning 'to vow,' arguing that a vote is by definition a vow of support. To deny that a vote is an alliance is to deny the basic meaning of the act being performed.
questions.quote: There’s a reason we call it “supporting” a candidate when you intend to vote for them. The etymology itself of the word “vote” betrays this. It comes from the root “vovere”, *to vow*. **You are vowing your support to him, come what may.**

questions.question: How does Weisser predict the trajectory of a 'lesser of two evils' vote over time?
questions.answer: He warns of a three-stage degradation: a 'lesser of two evils' is first tolerated, then preferred as evil, and finally redefined as good. Without confrontation, the compromise hardens into idolatry of the politician.
questions.quote: **A “lesser of two evils” will always become a “preferable evil”, and if left unconfronted, will become a “preferable good”.**

questions.question: What is Weisser's response to invoking Ordo Amoris to justify voting for a flawed candidate to protect one's own children?
questions.audience: Republican-leaning pro-life Christian who voted for Trump/Vance
questions.answer: He calls this an inversion of the doctrine into 'Ordo Neglectus.' Because all true love is rooted in love of God, and loving God means keeping His commandments, any 'love' that requires sin against the unborn is not ordered love at all but pagan affinity.
questions.quote: Let the truth be stated clearly: **“Love” that betrays the higher love of God is not love at all; it is mere pagan affinity.**

questions.question: How does Weisser address the 'I'm not voting for the abortion stuff, I'm voting for the other stuff' objection?
questions.audience: Republican-leaning pro-life Christian who voted for Trump/Vance
questions.answer: He compares it to a Congressman voting for an evil omnibus bill because of attached good provisions. Citing Romans 3:8, he argues no expected good can permit doing evil, and the rationalization is the same condemned slander.
questions.quote: By now, you may be realizing the problem, as stated earlier in this article: No amount of “good that may come” can permit an evil.

questions.question: Why does Weisser consider the overturning of Roe via Dobbs to be harmful rather than victorious?
questions.answer: Before Dobbs, abolitionists were pushing state nullification of Roe while pro-life groups defended regulation 'with compliance to Roe.' Dobbs let pro-life leaders drop the Roe-submission talking point and lulled Christians into believing the fight was won, while abortion numbers nationally have not meaningfully dropped.
questions.quote: Now that *Dobbs* is in place, and in many states abortion clinics have been closed, Christians all over the country are lulled into a feeling of accomplishment, as if we’ve won a massive victory over abortion. The fact of the matter is that abortion numbers have not gone down in any meaningful way, and have even gone up nationally.

questions.question: How does Weisser characterize the sin of presuming what God will do if Christians refuse to vote for a pro-abortion candidate?
questions.audience: Reformed Christian voter weighing 'lesser of two evils' arguments
questions.answer: He calls it the sin of presumption, paralleled by the ten faithless spies in Numbers, and worse—false prophecy of the Ezekiel 13 variety, where men 'prophesy smooth things from their own hearts' about the gains a compromise vote will bring.
questions.quote: This is the sin of **presumption**. You *presume* what will happen based on your human reason, and what God has commanded falls to second place in your heart, behind the fear you have of the future.

questions.question: Why does Weisser reject the 'you compromise too by buying from Amazon' objection?
questions.audience: Skeptic who sees voting as a private matter of conscience
questions.answer: He calls it a category error. Doing business with pagans is permitted in Scripture (Deuteronomy 2:6) and does not institute their laws or appoint pagan lawmakers, whereas voting directly installs evil rulers and frames evil statutes.
questions.quote: This is a category error. By doing business with pagans, you are not instituting their laws, or instituting a pagan lawmaker. The Old Testament allows Christians to do business with pagans.

questions.question: What does Weisser warn about politically connected sycophants within the abolitionist movement?
questions.answer: He warns that platform-seekers and political ladder-climbers who attack the prophetic voices of abolitionists rather than the candidate's evils are a Doeg-the-Edomite type, divisive by nature, and should not be heralded as examples of the movement.
questions.quote: I want to posit that as abolitionists, we should not platform or yolk ourselves to these men. Now, I’m not saying that we can’t work with folks who have voted for Trump. People will accuse me of that, but that’s not what I’m saying. I am saying that platform-seekers and political ladder-climbing types are not to be heralded as examples of our movement.

questions.question: How does Weisser frame the abolitionist's posture toward worldly pragmatism in voting?
questions.answer: He says abolitionism is providential rather than pragmatic, and that the most pragmatic action is always doing what God says, even when it appears unlikely to produce success—as illogical as marching around Jericho's walls thirteen times with trumpets.
questions.quote: There’s nothing pragmatic about marching around a wall thirteen times while blasting trumpets if you want that wall to be torn down. It doesn’t make sense with worldly wisdom. Rather, the most wise and most pragmatic thing to do at every given moment is *what God says to do,* and never *what God says not to do.*

related.title: How Shall an Abolitionist Vote?
related.reason: Sam Jones lays out the abolitionist voting framework triggered by Trump's 2024 abortion statement, providing the prior-occasion companion to Weisser's full theological argument.

related.title: Christians could force Trump to be an abolitionist of abortion with merely a lift of the finger. #YouDontGetMyVoteYET
related.reason: Earlier Weisser piece on the same Trump betrayal, advocating the 'withhold the vote until' strategy that this article theologically grounds.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Directly addresses the rape/incest exceptions Trump endorses, which Weisser cites as one of the candidate's pro-abortion betrayals.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Long-form critique of pro-life compromise that complements Weisser's broader thesis that lesser-evil voting is sin.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Echoes the addendum's warning against sycophants who silence prophetic critique in favor of political accommodation.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Tackles the incrementalist political theology underlying the lesser-of-two-evils voting Weisser denounces.

related.title: Against Pro-Life Compromise: Responding to Denny Burk, Andrew Walker, et al.
related.reason: Engages the same Reformed audience Weisser addresses and rebuts the conscience/compromise framing he answers in his objections section.
